subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B2.1.1.1 Count and estimate quantities from 0 to 1000
indicator_description: Use number names, counting sequences and how to count to find out "how many?"
examplars:
1. Skip count forwards and backwards to and from 1000 respectively by 2s, 5s and 10s, starting at 0 or at multiples of 2, 5, 10, 50 and 100; Identify and correct errors or omissions in counting or skip counting sequences [cite: 2670]
2. Count by 2s, 5s or 10s, to answer "how many?" questions about as many as 100 or 1000 objects [cite: 2670]
3. Represent the number of objects in a group with a written numeral to 1000 [cite: 2670]
4. Estimate the number of objects in a group of up to 1000 describe the estimation strategy used; Select an appropriate estimate among all those given and justify the choice [cite: 2670]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B2.1.1.1 Count and estimate quantities from 0 to 1000
indicator_description: Identify numbers in different positions around a given number in a number chart (1-1000)
examplars: 1. Display a number chart with numbers between 0 and 100 and have learners identify numbers in different positions around a given number. Put learners in convenient groups and give each group a number grid and have them identify numbers in different positions around a chosen number. For example, choose 18 and identify numbers above, below, to the right or to the left etc. [cite: 2676]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B2.1.1.1 Count and estimate quantities from 0 to 1000
indicator_description: Use number names and non-standard units (marked 10s and Is) for measuring (lengths and volumes) to count to find out "how long or how much?" up to 999
examplars:
1. Have learners use their feet, strides, arms, hand-span and referent materials such as sticks or threads to find how long a table, window and door frames etc., by counting the number of times their feet, hand-span and referent materials is able to do this [cite: 2676]
2. Have learners use empty container such as bottles, cups etc. to determine the capacity of other bigger containers by counting to find how much (the number of times) the bottles, cups etc. is able to do this [cite: 2676]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B2.1.1.1 Count and estimate quantities from 0 to 1000
indicator_description: Demonstrate a conceptual understanding of place value of whole numbers between 0 and 100
examplars:
1. Develop a conceptual understanding of place value of whole numbers between 0 and 1000 by: explain and show- with bundles of 10s and Is and a tens frame - the meaning of each digit in a 2-digit number (when the two digits are different, as well as when the two digits are the same) and representing the number in a tens frame [cite: 2679]
2. Decompose or partition numbers to 1000 into hundreds, tens and ones (e.g.: 153=100+50+3 or 153=100+53) [cite: 2679]
3. Explain why the value of a digit depends upon its placement within a numeral [cite: 2679]
4. Read a number by indicating the value of each digit (i.e., reading 43 as forty-three and not four three) [cite: 2679]
5. Partition or decompose numbers to 100 and then to 1000 into equivalent expressions (e.g.: 47=20+20+7, or 30+10+7 etc.) [cite: 2679]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B2.1.1.1 Count and estimate quantities from 0 to 1000
indicator_description: Represent number quantities up to 1000 in equivalent ways focusing on place value and equality
examplars:
1. Demonstrate an understanding of how place value determines the relative size of numbers up to 1000 by: describing the relative size of two or more numbers (i.e., saying whether one number is a little or a lot bigger or smaller than another and justifying the answer) [cite: 2682]
2. Represent and describe numbers to 1000 in equivalent ways (e.g., 147 is 3 less than 150, is 7 more than 140, is almost half of 300, is a little larger than 145 but a lot larger than 15, etc.) [cite: 2682]
3. Arrange groups of up to 100 objects in equal groups in more than 1 way and describe the arrangement; or describe the arrangement and how many are left over (e.g. arrange 66 as two groups of 33 or six groups of 11, or 3 groups of 22 or 5 groups of 12 and 6 left over) [cite: 2682]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B2.1.1.1 Count and estimate quantities from 0 to 1000
indicator_description: Use place value to compare and order whole numbers up to 100 using comparative language, numbers, and symbols (>, <, or =).
examplars:
1. Identify which of two given numbers is bigger (or smaller), explain why, and represent the relationship using the symbols < and > [cite: 2682]
2. Put a small group of numbers in increasing or decreasing order and justify the order using place value [cite: 2682]
3. Identify the missing numbers in a section of number line from I to 100 or in a hundreds chart and justifying the answer using place value [cite: 2682]
4. Solve word problems that involve comparing quantities up to 100 (i.e., Ahmed has 23 chickens. Amina has 46. What can you say?) [cite: 2682]

subject: MATHEMATICS
sub_strand: Number Operations (Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication and Division)
content_standard: B2.1.2.1 Demonstrate conceptual understanding of operations of addition and subtraction with sums up to 100
indicator_description: Use conceptual understanding of addition and subtraction to add, and subtract numbers to 100
examplars:
1. Add a given set of numbers in two different ways (e.g. 35 + 54 and 54 + 35 or 18 + 12 + 3 and 3 + 18 + 12) and explaining why the order in which numbers are added does not change the sum [cite: 2685]
2. Explain why the difference or sum is the same as the initial number when 0 is added or subtracted from a number (e.g., why 27 + 0 = 27 or 55 - 0 = 55) [cite: 2685]
3. Match a word problem to a missing addend (e.g., 34 + _ = 57), missing subtrahend I (e.g. 27 - _ = 24) or missing minuend 54 = 63 statement [cite: 2685]
4. Create an addition or subtraction number sentence and word problem for a number up to 100 (i.e., given the solution 53, create an addition or subtraction sentence with an answer of 53 and a corresponding word problem) [cite: 2685]

subject: MATHEMATICS
sub_strand: Number Operations (Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication and Division)
content_standard: B2.1.2.2 Demonstrate an understanding of the concept of "not equal to" to solve addition and subtraction problems with sums up to 100
indicator_description: Use the concept of "equal to" and "not equal to" to solve addition and subtraction problems with sums up 100
examplars:
1. Explaining that that "≠" means "not the same as" or "not equal to" [cite: 2685]
2. Constructing and drawing two sets that are not equal, explaining why they are not equal and recording the relationship using the symbol (e.g., ≠) [cite: 2685]
3. Changing two given sets, equal in size, to create sets that are not equal, explain the changes made and why [cite: 2685]
4. Determining whether two sides of a given number sentence are equal or not and using the appropriate symbol to represent the relationship (e.g., 16 ≠ 8 + 5) [cite: 2689]
5. Using a symbol (□) to represent an unknown in addition/subtraction statements to 100 [cite: 2689]
6. Demonstrate an understanding of the relationship between addition and subtraction by describing a subtraction as an equivalent addition and vice versa; i.e. finding the missing addend [cite: 2689]

subject: MATHEMATICS
sub_strand: Number Operations (Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication and Division)
content_standard: B2.1.2.3 Develop and use strategies for mentally computing basic additions and subtraction facts to 19
indicator_description: Use mental strategies for basic addition facts to 19 and related subtraction facts to 19
examplars:
1. Demonstrate fluency with addition and subtraction-related relationships by: Quickly naming numbers that are 1, 2, or 10 more or less than a number between I and 100, or 5 more than a number that is a multiple of 5 [cite: 2689]
2. Quickly Identifying the double of a number between and 12 [cite: 2689]
3. Add and subtract combinations to 10 quickly and accurately [cite: 2689]
4. Use strategies like 'counting up' when adding [cite: 2692]
5. Use 'making doubles' (e.g., instead of 4 + 6 use 5 + 5) [cite: 2692]
6. Use 'making doubles + or - 1' (e.g., instead of 4 + 5 use 4 + 4 + 1) [cite: 2692]
7. Use 'making 10s' (e.g., instead of 7 + 5 use 7 + 3 + 2) [cite: 2692]
8. Use 'counting down' for subtraction [cite: 2692]
9. Turning a subtraction into an addition (e.g., 7 - 3 = _ can be rephrased as 3 + _ = 7) [cite: 2692]

subject: MATHEMATICS
sub_strand: Number Operations (Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication and Division)
content_standard: B2.1.2.4 Develop and use conventional and personal strategies for computing additions up to 100
indicator_description: Use conventional strategy to add and subtract within 100
examplars: 1. Use objects (groups of tens and ones) or drawings to model addition and subtraction of I and 2 digit numbers (with answers within 100, with and without regrouping) and record the process symbolically, using an addition or subtraction frame [cite: 2695]

subject: MATHEMATICS
sub_strand: Number Operations (Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication and Division)
content_standard: B2.1.2.4 Develop and use conventional and personal strategies for computing additions up to 100
indicator_description: Use personal strategies to add and subtract within 100
examplars:
1. Decompose a number into easier numbers to add and doing partial sums- Decomposing one number into easier numbers to add [cite: 2695]
2. "Friendly jumps" strategy, where one of the numbers is decomposed into a friendlier number and added in "chunks" or by "friendly jumps" [cite: 2698]
3. Moving part of one number to the other number to create numbers that are easier to add (e.g. 29 + 56 = 30 + 55 = 85) [cite: 2698]
4. Compensation - Adding more to a number to make it friendlier, then subtracting the amount added from the answer [cite: 2698]

subject: MATHEMATICS
sub_strand: Number Operations (Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication and Division)
content_standard: B2.1.2.4 Develop and use conventional and personal strategies for computing additions up to 100
indicator_description: Solve one-step and multi-step word problems involving addition and subtraction within 100 using a variety of strategies based on place value, including algorithms.
examplars:
1. Word problems like: Yaw has 32 books. Aisha added 13 more books. How many books altogether? [cite: 2698]
2. Word problems like: Mr. Haruna gave 45 books to Yaa. She gave some to her sister. How many are left? [cite: 2698]
3. Word problems like: Araba needs 8 Ghana cedis. She has 5. How much more money does she need? [cite: 2698]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B2.1.3.1 Develop an understanding of halves and fourths using concrete and pictorial representations [Exclude notation here]
indicator_description: Understand the fraction one-half and one-quarter as the quantity obtained by taking I part when a whole is partitioned into two or four equal parts
examplars:
1. Use concrete objects to explain the fraction one-fourth as the quantity obtained by taking I part when a whole object is partitioned into four equal parts [cite: 2702]
2. Use pictorial representations to explain the fraction one-fourth [cite: 2702]
3. Use pictorial representations to help learners sort fractions into those that are halves and those that are one-fourths [cite: 2702]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B2.1.3.1 Develop an understanding of halves and fourths using concrete and pictorial representations [Exclude notation here]
indicator_description: Count in halves and quarters (fourths) using concrete and pictorial representations) of halves and fourths.
examplars:
1. Show several fourths of concrete objects and have them count them in fourths (using the language one-fourth, two-fourths, three-fourths, etc.) [cite: 2705]
2. Show learners several pictorial representations of fourths and have them count [cite: 2705]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B2.1.3.1 Develop an understanding of halves and fourths using concrete and pictorial representations [Exclude notation here]
indicator_description: Determine the number of halves and quarters in a whole
examplars: 1. Show learners several pictorial representations (or card cut outs) of halves, fourths and wholes and ask them to state the relationship between (i) a whole and one-half; (ii) a whole and one-fourth, and (iii) one-half and one-fourth. [cite: 2705]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B2.1.4.1. Determine the value of coins and notes in order to solve monetary transactions
indicator_description: Recognise Ghanaian coins, and currency notes to include at least I cedi, 2 cedis, 5 cedis, 10 cedis, 20 cedis and 50 cedis and determine the value of a collection of coins and notes up to at least 50 Ghana cedis
examplars:
1. Display the Ghanaian cedi (coins and notes) currently being used for transaction in Ghana and initiate discussion on the need for monetary transaction [cite: 2709]
2. Introduce the notes (i.e. I cedi, 2 cedis, 5 cedis 10 cedis etc.) in turns and have learners examine and talk about its features [cite: 2709]
3. State the relationship between ¢2 and ¢10; ¢5 and ¢10; ¢2 and ¢20; ¢5 and ¢20; ¢10 and ¢50 [cite: 2709]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B2.2.1.1 Recognize, create, extend, describe, and use patterns and rules to solve mathematical tasks
indicator_description: Demonstrate an understanding of increasing and decreasing number patterns
examplars:
1. Identify the pattern rule used to create a pattern that increases or decreases by 2, 5 or 10. (i.e. in the pattern 2, 4, 6, 8 the rule is "add 2 or jump by 2") and extend the pattern for the next 2 or 3 terms. [cite: 2712]
2. Identify errors or omissions in increasing or decreasing patterns and explain the reasoning and strategy used [cite: 2712]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B2.2.1.1 Recognize, create, extend, describe, and use patterns and rules to solve mathematical tasks
indicator_description: Identify, create and describe the rule for simple number patterns involving repeated addition or subtraction, skip counting and arrays of objects.
examplars:
1. Find the missing terms in numerical patterns like 9, 1, 3, 5, 9, 1, 3, 5, _ [cite: 2712]
2. Identify and describe the rules for patterns like 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, ... (the rule is "add two") [cite: 2712]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B2.3.1.1 Describe and analyse 2D shapes and 3D objects.
indicator_description: Identify the common features or attributes of a collection of 3D objects (spheres, cylinders, cones, pyramids, cubes) of different dimensions or orientations.
examplars:
1. Recognise and name 3D objects having specific features or attributes (number of equal faces, types of faces, number of corners, etc.) [cite: 2717]
2. Sort a collection of 3D objects by I or 2 features and explain the sorting rule use [cite: 2717]
3. Identify examples of these 3D objects in the classroom and community [cite: 2717]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B2.3.1.1 Describe and analyse 2D shapes and 3D objects.
indicator_description: Identify the common features or attributes of a collection of 2D shapes (squares, triangles, rectangles, circles, pentagons, hexagons) of different dimensions or orientations
examplars:
1. Recognize, draw and name 2D shapes having specific features or attributes (for example a given number of angles or edges/sides) [cite: 2717]
2. Sort a collection of 2D shapes by I or 2 features or attributes and explain the sorting rule used [cite: 2717]
3. Identify examples of these 2D shapes in the classroom and community [cite: 2717]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B2.3.1.1 Describe and analyse 2D shapes and 3D objects.
indicator_description: Create two-dimensional shapes based on given attributes, including number of sides and vertices.
examplars:
1. Identify the 2D faces of a given 3D object [cite: 2722]
2. Identify 3D objects in the environment that have parts similar to a given 2D shape [cite: 2722]
3. Learners identify the number of sides, faces and vertices of 2D plane figures [cite: 2722]
4. Learners draw 2D shapes with given sides or vertices [cite: 2722]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B2.3.2.1 Demonstrate that the length of an object does not change with its placement or direction
indicator_description: Prove that the placement or direction of a shape or object does not change its length.
examplars: 1. Put sticks of equal length in different places and directions and ask learners to identify the longest; then pick the sticks and put them side by side to see if they have equal length. [cite: 2726]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B2.3.3.1 Use non-standard units for measuring lengths, heights, mass and distance around objects
indicator_description: Demonstrate an understanding of how to measure lengths, capacities or mass - directly or indirectly using non-standard units
examplars:
1. Identifying which of two non-standard units would be a better choice for measuring (e.g., thumb width, paper clips, pencil lengths) [cite: 2729]
2. Explain why the number of non-standard units an object measures varies depending upon the size of the non-standard unit used [cite: 2729]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B2.3.3.1 Use non-standard units for measuring lengths, heights, mass and distance around objects
indicator_description: Develop an understanding of measuring as a process of comparing three or more items
examplars:
1. Selecting an appropriate non-standard unit for measuring, estimating, and comparing the estimate with the actual measure [cite: 2732]
2. Comparing and ordering objects by length, height, distance around, capacity or mass (weight), using non-standard units, and describing the relative size of the objects [cite: 2732]
3. Learners bring together several collection of objects and compare using words such as smaller, smallest, longer, longest, bigger, biggest, heavier, heaviest etc. [cite: 2732]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B2.3.3.2 Use standard units to measure lengths, heights, mass and distance around objects.
indicator_description: Recognize the need for standard unit of measurement of length
examplars:
1. Mark a learner's height on the wall and ask 4 learners to use their hand span to measure. Establish the need for standard units based on different obtained measures [cite: 2736]
2. Estimate the measure in non-standard units, and then comparing the estimate with the actual measure [cite: 2736]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B2.3.3.3 Develop an understanding of the measurement of time taken by events using arbitrary units and the hour
indicator_description: Read the calendar and solve problems involving the number of days in a week and number of months in a year.
examplars:
1. Identify or read the day of the week and the month of the year for a given calendar date [cite: 2739]
2. Identify the day (or month) that comes before or after a given day (or month) [cite: 2739]
3. Name, order and count the days in a week and the months in a year [cite: 2739]
4. Ask learners to say the rhyme "Thirty-days has September" [cite: 2739]

subject: MATHEMATICS
content_standard: B2.3.3.3 Develop an understanding of the measurement of time taken by events using arbitrary units and the hour
indicator_description: Use arbitrary units and hour on the clock to measure time to complete simple events.
examplars:
1. Tell how much time (in terms of arbitrary units like claps, water timers, etc.) it would take to walk round the classroom or sing a song [cite: 2739]
2. Describe events that take an hour or more or less than an hour [cite: 2739]
3. Ask learners to watch the clock each hour and note how long they stay in school each day [cite: 2739]

subject: MATHEMATICS
sub_strand: Data Collection, Organisation, Presentation, Interpretation and Analysis
content_standard: B2.4.1.1 Collect and record data about self and others and use it to answer and pose questions
indicator_description: Use tallies, checkmarks, charts, lists or objects to collect and organize data to answer and pose questions about themselves, others, or surroundings.
examplars:
1. "What is our favourite food or colour or sport?" [cite: 2744]
2. Answer and/or pose questions, and justify the answers, based on the organized data [cite: 2744]

subject: MATHEMATICS
sub_strand: Data Collection, Organisation, Presentation, Interpretation and Analysis
content_standard: B2.4.1.2 Construct and interpret concrete graphs and pictographs
indicator_description: Draw and interpret concrete graphs and pictographs
examplars:
1. Use one-to-many correspondence to create concrete graphs or pictographs to represent data collected [cite: 2744]
2. Using a one-to-one correspondence solve simple problems (how many altogether, comparing, or take apart problems) on concrete graphs or pictographs [cite: 2744]
